title: Hands-On: Metamorphic Testing
concept:
This exercise develops practical proficiency in applying metamorphic testing to an ML system. The four-step process is: (1) derive metamorphic relations from domain knowledge, (2) generate source test cases, (3) create follow-up test cases by applying the MRs, and (4) run and verify the MRs hold.

**System**: A health risk ML model that predicts expected age at death. Inputs: `patient_name`, `age_current`, `cigarettes_per_day`, `exercise_hours_per_week`, `bmi`, `systolic_bp`.

**Step 1: Derive MRs from domain knowledge**
- MR1 (Monotonicity): More smoking → lower predicted age. If `cigarettes_per_day` increases, predicted_age should decrease or stay equal.
- MR2 (Invariance): Name change → same predicted age. If `patient_name` changes, prediction must be identical.
- MR3 (Monotonicity): More exercise → higher predicted age. If `exercise_hours_per_week` increases, predicted_age should increase or stay equal.

**Step 2: Source test case**
- Input: {name: 'Alice', age: 45, cigarettes: 10, exercise: 3, bmi: 24.5, bp: 120}
- Output: 78 years

**Step 3: Generate follow-up test cases**
- Follow-up 1 (MR1): cigarettes: 25, all else same → expected: ≤ 78 years
- Follow-up 2 (MR2): name: 'Bob', all else same → expected: = 78 years
- Follow-up 3 (MR3): exercise: 8, all else same → expected: ≥ 78 years

**Step 4: Verify**
If any follow-up result violates the MR, investigate whether the MR definition is correct (domain knowledge check) or whether the model has a defect.
keyPoints: Metamorphic relations must be derived from domain knowledge or scientific laws — they are the test design artefact, not just intuitions.
Follow-up test cases are created by applying the MR transformation to the source input; only the source output (not a new oracle) is needed to verify the relation.
A violated MR requires investigation of both the MR definition and the model — either could be wrong.

quiz.question: A tester applies MR2 (changing patient name should not affect predicted age) and finds the model predicts 72 years for 'Bob' but 78 years for 'Alice' with identical other inputs. What are the two possible conclusions?
options: The model is correct because names can correlate with demographics affecting longevity.

The MR is violated. Either the model has a defect (the name feature is incorrectly influencing predictions) OR the MR is incorrectly defined (names do carry relevant information in this model's design). The team must investigate the model's feature importance to determine which.
The model is correct because MR2 only applies to invariance of continuous features, not categorical names.
The result is acceptable because a ±6 year difference is within the model's margin of error.
quiz.explanation: A violated MR has two possible root causes: a model defect or an incorrectly specified MR. For this case, if the model uses name as a feature (perhaps as a proxy for gender or ethnicity), the MR definition is incorrect. If name is not a feature, the model has a defect. Investigating feature importance in the model reveals which is true. The ±6 year difference is not a precision issue — the MR requires invariance (identical output).

exercise.scenario: A Machine Learning System (MLS) predicts expected age at death based on patient lifestyle and health factors. Inputs: patient_name (string), age_current (integer, years), cigarettes_per_day (integer), exercise_hours_per_week (float), bmi (float), systolic_bp (integer).

relations.name: MR1 — Monotonicity (Smoking)
relations.description: Increasing cigarettes_per_day from 10 to 25, holding all other inputs constant, should produce a predicted age ≤ 78 years. More smoking must not increase predicted lifespan.

relations.name: MR2 — Invariance (Patient Name)
relations.description: Changing patient_name from 'Alice Smith' to 'Bob Jones', holding all other inputs constant, must produce the same predicted age of 78 years. Name is an irrelevant feature for longevity prediction.

relations.name: MR3 — Monotonicity (Exercise)
relations.description: Increasing exercise_hours_per_week from 3 to 8, holding all other inputs constant, should produce a predicted age ≥ 78 years. More exercise must not decrease predicted lifespan.

relations.name: MR4 — Invariance (Unit Consistency)
relations.description: Expressing age_current as 45 years vs an equivalent model input should produce the same output. Equivalent representations of the same value should yield identical predictions.
